Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd dedicate big chunks of time when I could focus, usually around 23 hours a week, but it wasn't evenly spread out. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to recorded lectures while doing other things, or I'd talk through concepts out loud to myself. My attendance was really low because I found I could learn better on my own terms, downloading materials and working through them at my own pace.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on. I have extracurriculars that take up significant time, so I'd often have to shift my studying around those commitments. When deadlines approached, I'd intensify my focus, but otherwise I tried to maintain a steady baseline of about 23 hours weekly. The anxiety about keeping up pushed me to stay somewhat consistent even when motivation was low.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check for new assignments or download lecture recordings. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they made things feel less monotonous. I'd also check discussion boards occasionally to see if anyone had asked questions I was also wondering about. But honestly, I didn't log in just to browse—it was always pretty purposeful.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd go in bursts—download what I needed, then work offline for days. The gamified elements did make me check in more than I otherwise would have, which was kind of clever. But routine? That wasn't my relationship with it.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over two hours worth of content because hearing explanations really helps things click for me. I also found the discussion posts useful for clarifying concepts I was uncertain about. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful because they broke down bigger concepts into manageable pieces and made studying feel less like a chore.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screen for hours gave me headaches. I'd skim them at best. Some of the peer interaction features I also underused—I only had about four interactions because I preferred to work through things independently or with people I knew outside the platform.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I'd be tested on, or if it made a confusing concept clearer. The gamified tasks were good at this—they gave immediate feedback so I knew if I was on track. Anything that felt like busywork or repetition of stuff I already understood, I'd skip. Time is precious when you're juggling a lot.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my anxiety levels, I'd stress about whether skipping them would hurt me, but my low motivation made it hard to push through if they weren't required. Required activities got done no matter what because the stakes were clear. Optional ones I'd only tackle if they seemed genuinely useful or if the gamification made them feel rewarding.
Q9: Absolutely, this was basically my main mode of studying. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I took handwritten notes because writing helps me process information even though I learn best by listening. I also used free online resources—YouTube videos, podcasts about the subject matter—that wouldn't show up anywhere in the LMS.
Q10: Most of my studying happened offline, honestly. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, talk through problems out loud, and review my handwritten notes for hours. None of that shows up in any log. There were entire weeks where my LMS activity looked dead but I was actually deeply engaged with the material, just not in ways the system could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, then replay it five or six times offline over the following days. Same with any PDFs I did bother saving—I'd access them once through the LMS, then they lived on my phone or laptop. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than any log would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when my anxiety spiked about whether I was doing enough. Paradoxically, high anxiety sometimes made it harder to actually engage because I'd feel paralyzed. When extracurriculars got intense or when I was dealing with limited money for basics, focusing on coursework felt impossible. My resilience usually got me through, but those patches were rough.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. I'd open videos and let them play without truly listening. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to triage—focus on what would directly impact my grade and let other things slide. The fatigue was real.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, watching a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere—worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd have to rewatch content later when I could actually concentrate. The system would show I watched something, but the meaningful engagement happened on the second or third viewing, usually offline.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate wasn't great even though my exam scores were high. The anxiety made starting things feel overwhelming sometimes. I knew they were important, but I'd convince myself I'd do better work if I waited until I felt more prepared. It was a coping mechanism that didn't always serve me well.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator, honestly. I'd ramp up my LMS activity in the days before something was due, downloading everything I needed and then working intensively offline. Without deadlines, my engagement would drift. They created necessary pressure, though they also fed into my anxiety cycle.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on exams, it boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage. Knowing my approach was working despite low attendance validated my independent study style. Negative feedback on assignments made me anxious but also pushed me to pay more attention to those areas. The gamified task feedback was nice because it was immediate.
Q18: The course structure actually worked okay for me because there was flexibility in how I could engage. I could skip lectures and still access recordings, which suited my style. The 12 online courses I was managing meant I needed that flexibility. However, the structure didn't do much to motivate consistent participation—it was pretty hands-off, which suited me but probably wasn't ideal.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they added some fun to otherwise dry material. The availability of video content was crucial for my auditory learning style. What made it harder was when things required synchronous participation or heavy reading; those didn't match how I learn best. Also, the peer interaction features felt forced and awkward.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work—like they're preparing but can't pull the trigger on submissions. Or they might be genuinely engaging with content but struggling with the actual assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxious clicking around.
Q21: That's literally me. My LMS engagement was low, but I got a Distinction because I was learning effectively offline. I'd download materials, study independently, and really master the content for exams. The logs would show minimal activity, but my exam score of 94.58 tells a different story. Some of us just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn well.
Q22: Logs can show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and maybe surface-level participation in discussions. But they completely miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they watched, how much they studied offline, or the quality of their thinking. They measure presence, not learning. My logs would make me look disengaged when I was anything but.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me. It's not about logging in or clicking through videos—it's about whether the concepts are sticking, whether I can apply them, whether I'm actually learning. For me, engagement often looks like pacing around my room talking through ideas out loud, which no system would ever capture.
Q24: So much. My anxiety about the course, which actually pushed me to study harder even when I wasn't logging in. The hours I spent listening to lectures offline, talking through concepts, making connections between ideas. My resilience in pushing through tough moments. The fact that I was genuinely happy with my learning despite what the metrics might suggest. The LMS only sees a shadow of what was really happening.
